printNote: Keep this cheat sheet handy when setting up a new Python environment.

entries.description: Check Python is installed and on PATH. Shows version number.
entries.commonMistake: Forgetting to open a NEW terminal after installation — old terminal doesn't see updated PATH

entries.description: Check pip is available. The path confirms which Python it belongs to.
entries.commonMistake: pip and python3 pointing to different Python versions — use 'python3 -m pip' instead

entries.description: Quick smoke test — runs a Python expression inline without creating a file.
entries.commonMistake: Using single quotes inside the string when the outer string also uses single quotes — escape them or swap quote types

entries.example:
$ python3 -m venv .venv
# Creates .venv/ directory with isolated Python + pip
entries.description: Create an isolated virtual environment in the .venv directory.
entries.commonMistake: Creating the venv but forgetting to activate it — packages still install globally

entries.description: Activate the virtual environment on macOS/Linux. Prompt changes to show (.venv).
entries.commonMistake: On Windows the command is: .venv\Scripts\activate (backslash, no 'source')

entries.description: Activate virtual environment on Windows (Command Prompt).
entries.commonMistake: Running this in PowerShell may require: Set-ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -Scope CurrentUser

entries.description: Exit (deactivate) the active virtual environment. Works on all platforms.
entries.commonMistake: Closing the terminal without deactivating — the venv is automatically deactivated when the terminal closes

entries.example:
(.venv) $ pip install requests
Successfully installed requests-2.31.0
entries.description: Install a package from PyPI. Always run inside an activated virtual environment.
entries.commonMistake: Running pip install without activating venv — installs globally and potentially causes conflicts

entries.example:
(.venv) $ pip freeze > requirements.txt
# requirements.txt now contains: requests==2.31.0 ...
entries.description: Save all installed packages with exact versions to requirements.txt.
entries.commonMistake: Using 'pip list' instead of 'pip freeze' — pip list shows human-readable output, not installable format

entries.example:
(.venv) $ pip install -r requirements.txt
# Installs all packages at pinned versions
entries.description: Install all packages listed in requirements.txt. Used to reproduce an environment.
entries.commonMistake: Forgetting to activate the venv before running this — installs into system Python

entries.example:
>>> import sys
>>> sys.version_info
sys.version_info(major=3, minor=12, micro=0, ...)
entries.description: Named tuple with Python version details. Access .major, .minor, .micro.
entries.commonMistake: Using sys.version (a string) instead of sys.version_info (a tuple) for version comparisons in code

entries.description: Absolute path to the Python interpreter currently running. Useful for subprocess calls.
entries.commonMistake: Hardcoding '/usr/bin/python3' in scripts — use sys.executable to always get the correct interpreter

entries.description: List of directories Python searches for modules. '' means the current directory.
entries.commonMistake: Manually appending to sys.path in production code — instead, structure your project correctly or install as a package
